description: Broad evidence categories for compressing Stack Overflow posting histories before mapping to the MatrAIx persona schema.

evidence_categories.label: Technical expertise
evidence_categories.description: Programming languages, frameworks, tools, and domains demonstrated through the tags, vocabulary, and depth of questions and answers.

evidence_categories.label: Topic interests
evidence_categories.description: Recurring tags, technologies, and subject areas the user engages with over time.

evidence_categories.label: Problem-solving style
evidence_categories.description: How the user formulates problems and evaluates solutions, such as debugging rigor, research-before-asking, tradeoff analysis, or preference for minimal examples.

evidence_categories.label: Learning behavior
evidence_categories.description: Asking versus answering balance, self-answering, follow-up edits, and how the user acquires and shares knowledge.

evidence_categories.label: Communication style
evidence_categories.description: Writing patterns in public technical prose, such as thoroughness, structure, directness, tone, and courtesy.

evidence_categories.label: Values and motivations
evidence_categories.description: Grounded priorities reflected in posting behavior, such as helpfulness, correctness-seeking, craftsmanship, pragmatism, or knowledge-sharing.

evidence_categories.label: Work context
evidence_categories.description: Work practices visible in posts, such as production-system contexts, team workflows, tooling stacks, or deadline pressure mentioned while asking or answering.

evidence_categories.label: Explicit self-statements
evidence_categories.description: Direct statements about the user, such as job, education, location, health context, or life circumstances. These must be quoted directly and must not be inferred from technical activity alone.

direct_only_note: Demographic, health condition, family status, occupation, geography, political affiliation, religious identity, and identity-like attributes require explicit quoted self-statements. Repeated technical or topical engagement can be preserved as skills, interests, preferences, or values, but should not be converted into asserted sensitive identity/status labels without explicit evidence.
